content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

April 19, 1982


The Honorable John Thurman
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Assemblyman Thurman:

Pursuant to your request, this letter is written to
advise you on your reporting requirements under the Political
Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.1/

You are planning to retire at the end of your current
term. As you know, you are required to file a Leaving Office
Statement of Economic Interests, Form 721, within 30 days
after leaving public office. Section 87204. On this state-
ment, all investments, interests in real property, and income
during the period from January 1, 1982, to the end of your
term, must be disclosed. You requested advice on the method
of reporting of gifts given in anticipation of your retirement.

All sources of gifts aggregating $25 or more in value
must be disclosed on Schedule F. In addition to the name
and address of the donor, you must disclose a general descrip-
tion of the business activity, if any, of the donor, the
value of the gift, and the date on which it was received.
Section 87207. If a gift is made through an intermediary or
an agent, the full name, street address and business activity,
if any, of the intermediary or agent must also be disclosed.
Section 87210.

You also requested information on the reporting require-
ments for two specific gifts you anticipate receiving before
the end of your term.

The first gift is a testimonial dinner to be hosted by
friends and supporters. The tickets for the dinner are $10
each. Approximately 20% of the proceeds, or $2 from each
ticket sold, will be used to pay for a trip to Hawaii for

_______________________

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

The Honorable John Thurman
April 19, 1982
Page Two


you and your wife. There are actually two gifts here: the
dinner and the trip. The value of the dinner for reporting
purposes is reached by dividing the actual cost of the event
by the number of persons who attend. See Opinion requested
by Annie M. Gutierrez, 3 FPPC Opinions 44. (No. 76-081,
June 7, 1977). In this case, the cost of the dinner per
person appears to be approximately $8. Since this clearly
falls short of the $25 reporting threshold, you have no
reporting obligations as to the dinner.

As to the trip to Hawaii, you do have a reporting obligation since the value of the gift clearly exceeds $25. If no
one individual contributes $25 or more to this gift, you
must report the receipt of the gift, but you need describe
the donors only in general terms. See Opinion requested by
Assemblyman Art Torres, 2 FPPC Opinions 31 (No. 75-153,
February 4, 1976). However, anyone who gives more than $25
towards the gift by purchasing multiple tickets must be
identified on your Statement.

The second situation you inquired about involves a
parade to be given in your honor on John Thurman Day. The
proceeds from the parade will be donated to a hospital in
your name. You have no reporting obligations based on this
donation. Only payments received by a public official may
be gifts. Section 82028. Contributions given in an official's
name to a charitable organization are not gifts within the
meaning of the Act.

I would like to note that Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists
from making gifts totaling more than $10 in a calendar month
to any legislator. Accordingly, if $2 from each ticket is a
gift to you, a lobbyist may not buy more than five tickets
to your testimonial dinner without being in violation of
this prohibition.

I trust this discussion has been helpful to you. If I
can be of further assistance or you would like to discuss
this letter, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Diane Maura Wishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:km

sections.question: You requested advice on the method of reporting of gifts given in anticipation of your retirement. You also requested information on the reporting requirements for two specific gifts you anticipate receiving before the end of your term. The first gift is a testimonial dinner to be hosted by friends and supporters. The tickets for the dinner are $10 each. Approximately 20% of the proceeds, or $2 from each ticket sold, will be used to pay for a trip to Hawaii for you and your wife. The second situation you inquired about involves a parade to be given in your honor on John Thurman Day. The proceeds from the parade will be donated to a hospital in your name.
sections.conclusion: As to the trip to Hawaii, you do have a reporting obligation since the value of the gift clearly exceeds $25. If no one individual contributes $25 or more to this gift, you must report the receipt of the gift, but you need describe the donors only in general terms. However, anyone who gives more than $25 towards the gift by purchasing multiple tickets must be identified on your Statement. The second situation you inquired about involves a parade to be given in your honor on John Thurman Day. The proceeds from the parade will be donated to a hospital in your name. You have no reporting obligations based on this donation. Only payments received by a public official may be gifts. Contributions given in an official's name to a charitable organization are not gifts within the meaning of the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Assemblyman Thurman requested guidance on reporting requirements for gifts received in anticipation of his retirement, specifically regarding a testimonial dinner with proceeds funding a Hawaii trip and a parade where donations go to a charitable organization.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Hawaii trip must be reported as a gift exceeding $25, with donors identified if they individually contribute $25 or more; the testimonial dinner itself falls below the $25 reporting threshold. The parade donations to the hospital in his name are not reportable gifts since contributions to charitable organizations are not gifts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers but contains clear question and conclusion content. References multiple prior FPPC opinions. Includes additional guidance on lobbyist gift limitations under Section 86203.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You requested advice on the method of reporting of gifts given in anticipation of your retirement. You also requested information on the reporting requirements for two specific gifts you anticipate receiving before the end of your term. The first gift is a testimonial dinner to be hosted by friends and supporters. The tickets for the dinner are $10 each. Approximately 20% of the proceeds, or $2 from each ticket sold, will be used to pay for a trip to Hawaii for you and your wife. The second situation you inquired about involves a parade to be given in your honor on John Thurman Day. The proceeds from the parade will be donated to a hospital in your name.

CONCLUSION: As to the trip to Hawaii, you do have a reporting obligation since the value of the gift clearly exceeds $25. If no one individual contributes $25 or more to this gift, you must report the receipt of the gift, but you need describe the donors only in general terms. However, anyone who gives more than $25 towards the gift by purchasing multiple tickets must be identified on your Statement. The second situation you inquired about involves a parade to be given in your honor on John Thurman Day. The proceeds from the parade will be donated to a hospital in your name. You have no reporting obligations based on this donation. Only payments received by a public official may be gifts. Contributions given in an official's name to a charitable organization are not gifts within the meaning of the Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Diane Mauriello advises Assemblyman John Thurman on his Leaving Office Statement of Economic Interests filing requirements, specifically addressing the reporting obligations for gifts received in anticipation of his retirement, including a testimonial dinner/Hawaii trip and a parade fundraiser.
